coredata.dc:title: Green Synthesis of Fluorescent Carbon Quantum Dots for Detection of Hg2+
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2014 Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.

coredata.dc:creator: XU, Yue
TANG, Chun-Jing
HUANG, Hong
SUN, Chao-Qun
ZHANG, Ya-Kun
YE, Qun-Feng
WANG, Ai-Jun
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal treatment with apple juice as raw material. The result indicated that the fluorescence could be quenched by Hg2+ with high specificity. Based on this phenomenon, a selective and sensitive sensor was constructed for the detection of Hg2+ in phosphate solutions (pH 7.0). The fluorescence intensity showed linear responses with Hg2+ concentration ranging from 5.0 to 100.0 nM and 1.0 to 50.0 μM, with the detection limit of 2.3 nM (S/N = 3). The as-fabricated sensor was further extended for the determination of Hg2+ in real water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Green synthesis
Apple juice
Hg2+

xocs:doc.xocs:rawtext: CHINESE JOURNAL OF ANALYTICAL CHEMISTRY Volume 42, Issue 9, September 2014 Online English edition of the Chinese language journal Cite this article as: Chin J Anal Chem, 2014, 42(9), 1252â€“1258. Received 17 February 2014; accepted 1 May 2014 * Corresponding author. Email: ajwang@zjnu.cn This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21175118, 21275130), and the National Studentsâ€™ Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program of Zhejiang Normal University, China (No. 201310345020 for Y.X.). Copyright Â© 2014, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences. Published by Elsevier Limited. All rights reserved. DOI: 10.1016/S1872-2040(14)60765-9 RESEARCH PAPER Green Synthesis of Fluorescent Carbon Quantum Dots for Detection of Hg 2+ XU Yue 1 , TANG Chun-Jing 1 , HUANG Hong 2 , SUN Chao-Qun 1 , ZHANG Ya-Kun 1 , YE Qun-Feng 1 , WANG Ai-Jun 1, * 1 College of Geography and Environmental Science, Zhejiang Normal University, Jinhua 321004, China 2 College of Chemistry and Life Science, Zhejiang Normal University, Jinhua 321004, China Abstract: Fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal treatment with apple juice as raw material. The result indicated that the fluorescence could be quenched by Hg 2+ with high specificity. Based on this phenomenon, a selective and sensitive sensor was constructed for the detection of Hg 2+ in phosphate solutions (pH 7.0). The fluorescence intensity showed linear responses with Hg 2+ concentration ranging from 5.0 to 100.0 nM and 1.0 to 50.0 Î¼M, with the detection limit of 2.3 nM (S/N = 3). The as-fabricated sensor was further extended for the determination of Hg 2+ in real water samples. Key Words: Carbon quantum dots; Green synthesis; Apple juice; Hg 2+ 1 Introduction Mercury ion (Hg 2+ ) is one of the most hazardous metal ions and can accumulate in human body through the food chain, which causes toxicity to brain and central nervous system, kidney disease, and DNA damage [1] . In this regard, the development of a selective and sensitive method for the determination of Hg 2+ become a hot topic among researchers. Recently, several methods were developed for the detection of Hg 2+ such as colorimetric spectroscopy [2] , electrochemical methods [3] , fluorescent spectroscopy [4] , and atomic emission spectroscopy [5] . Compared with other sensing strategies, fluorescence technique was superior in the aspects of high sensitivity and selectivity, simplicity, and wide linear range. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with diameters below 10 nm are a new kind of carbon-based nanomaterials [6] . Compared with traditional organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots, CQDs show distinguished properties such as stable photoluminescence, easy surface functionalization, good biocompatibility, and low toxicity [7,8] . Thus, CQDs were widely applied in bioimaging [9] , sensing [10,11] , drug delivery [12] , and photocatalysis [13] . In recent years, many methods were developed for the synthesis of CQDs, including arc discharge [14] , laser ablation [15] , electrochemical [16] , chemical oxidation [17] , hydrothermal treatment [18] , ultrasonic [19] , and microwave methods [20] . Among them, hydrothermal synthesis was considered to be a simple and efficient way to prepare CQDs. For example, Sunâ€™s group prepared nitrogen-doped CQDs by hydrothermal treatment of grass, which was applied for the detection of Cu 2+ . Herein, fluorescent CQDs with blue fluorescence were prepared by one-pot hydrothermal treatment in which apple juice was used as raw material. The as-obtained CQDs were used for rapid detection of Hg 2+ in environmental water samples (Fig.1). 2 Experimental 2.1 Instruments Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) analysis was XU Yue et al. / Chinese Journal of Analytical Chemistry, 2014, 42(9): 1252â€“1258 2.2 2.4 2.6 2.8 3.0 3.2 0 10 20 30 P e r c e n t a g e / % Diameter / nm B 10 nm 0.205 nm A Fig.1 Preparation of CQDs and their application for Hg 2+ detection performed on a JEOL-2010F transmission electron microscope using an acceleration voltage of 200 kV. UV-vis absorption spectra were recorded on a Lambda 950 UV-vis spectrophotometer (Perkin-Elmer). Fluorescence spectroscopy experiments were conducted on a LS-45 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Perkin-Elmer). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) measurements were performed with a Nicolet NEXUS 670 FTIR spectrometer in the form of KBr pellets. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out by using a thermoelectron instrument (Thermo Scientific ESCALAB 250) with Al KÎ± X-ray radiation as the excitation source (1486.8 eV, 500 Âµm). 2.2 Materials Apples were purchased from local supermarket and processed into juice using an electrical fruit juicer before use. CdCl 2 , ZnCl 2 , FeCl 2 , MnCl 2 , BaCl 2 , CuCl 2 , CoCl 2 , CaCl 2 , KCl, FeCl 3 , MgCl 2 , PbCl 2 , NiCl 2 , NaCl, CH 3 COONa, NaNO 3 , Hg(NO 3 ) 2 and NaClO 4 were purchased from Aladdin Ltd. HgCl 2 and (CH 3 COO) 2 Hg were obtained from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Hg(ClO 4 ) 2 were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. All the other chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received. All the aqueous solutions were prepared with twice-distilled water. 2.3 Preparation of CQDs In a typical experiment, 35 mL of apple juice was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 Â°C for 12 h. Next, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature naturally. The large particles were removed from the product by filtration with a 0.22-Î¼m filter membrane and then centrifugated at 15000 rpm for 30 min. The precipitate was dried in vacuum for 72 h and re-dispersed in water with a concentration of 1 mg mL â€’1 , and kept at 4 Â°C for further characterization. 2.4 Detection of Hg 2+ Typically, 5 Î¼L of the CQD suspension (1 mg mL â€’1 ) was put into 1 mL phosphate solution (25 mM, pH 7.0), followed by the addition of different concentrations of Hg 2+ . After reaction for 10 min, the corresponding fluorescence spectra were recorded at room temperature. 3 Results and discussion 3.1 Characterization of CQDs As shown in Fig.2A, the TEM image illustrated that the typical product contained a lot of well-dispersed spherical particles. The CQDs had narrow size distribution by judging from 100 random particles with the average diameter of (2.8 Â± 0.4) nm, as clearly displayed in the size distribution histogram (Fig.2B). Furthermore, high-resolution TEM image showed the clear lattice plane with an inter-planer distance of 0.205 nm, corresponding to the (102) crystal planes of sp 2 graphitic carbon [22] . Fig.2 (A) TEM image and (B) the corresponding size distribution histogram. Inset: high resolution TEM image of CQDs XU Yue et al. / Chinese Journal of Analytical Chemistry, 2014, 42(9): 1252â€“1258 The CQDs suspension was clear yellow in visible light and exhibits a bright blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light (insets in Fig.3A), which was well consistent with those of CQDs obtained from strawberry juice [23] . The UV-vis absorption spectrum (Fig.3A) of CQDs exhibited a sharp peak at 283 nm, which probably originated from the Ï€-Ï€* transition of the conjugated C=C bond [22] . This observation was different from that of freshly apple juice with a peak at 262 nm (curve a). The maximum emission and excitation peaks of CQDs were located at 428 and 340 nm, respectively, but no fluorescence was detected from the apple juice. These results indicated the essential role of hydrothermal treatment for preparation of CQDs. As known, the main constituents of apple juice are carbohydrates such as glucose, sucrose, fructose, and ascorbic acid. Therefore, the formation of CQDs might involve dehydration, polymerization, and carbonization of the carbohydrates [24] , which was similar to those prepared in the literature [25,26] . As displayed in Fig.3B, varying the excitation wavelength from 330 to 430 nm, the emission peak was gradually red shifted from 426 to 502 nm, accompanied by the decrease of the fluorescence peak intensity. It means that CQDs show an excitation dependent emission. By using quinine sulfate (54%, 0.1 M H 2 SO 4 ) as a reference, the fluorescence quantum yield of CQDs was calculated to be 6.4%. The structure and functional groups of CQDs were characterized by FTIR and XPS spectroscopy. As shown in the survey XPS spectrum (Fig.4A), two strong peaks were observed at 533.6 and 283.3 eV, which were attributed to O 1s and C 1S , respectively, showing that CQDs mainly contain C and O elements. Fig.3 (A) UV-Vis absorption (a, b) and fluorescence (excitation spectrum: c, d; emission spectrum: e, f) spectra of apple juice before (a, c, e) and after (b, d, f) hydrothermal treatment. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of CQDs obtained at different excitation wavelengths. Insets show the photographs of CQDs in aqueous solution under visible light (1) and UV light (2) Fig.4 (A) Survey, (B) high-resolution C 1s and (C) O 1s XPS spectra of CQDs; (D) The corresponding FTIR spectra XU Yue et al. / Chinese Journal of Analytical Chemistry, 2014, 42(9): 1252â€“1258 Specifically, the deconvolution of high resolution C 1s spectrum (Fig.4B) showed four representative peaks at 284.8, 286.2, 287.9, and 289.1 eV, which were assigned to Câ€•C, Câ€•O, C=O, and Oâ€•C=O, respectively [23] . Meanwhile, the deconvolution of the O 1s spectrum (Fig.4C) revealed two typical peaks emerged at 532.7 and 531.9 eV, which were come from Câ€•OH/Câ€•Oâ€•C and C=O, respectively [27] . As shown in Fig.4D, the FTIR spectra of CQDs revealed the characteristic absorption bands of Oâ€•H and C=O stretching vibrations at 3405 and 1720 cm â€“1 , respectively. And the absorption bands at 1670 and 1075 cm â€“1 were originated from the stretching vibrations of C=C and Câ€•O groups, respectively [28] . The FTIR data agreed well with the above XPS analysis, revealing that the present oxygen-containing functional groups endowed CQDs with excellent water solubility. The stability of CQDs was investigated under different experimental conditions. The effects of NaCl concentration on the fluorescence intensity were firstly examined (Fig.5A). It was found that the fluorescence intensity of CQDs remained almost constant in the presence of different NaCl concentrations (up to 1.0 M). Moreover, as the pH values varied from 3.0 to 11.0 in the present system, the fluorescence of CQDs was insensitive to the pH values (Fig.5B), and only a slight change of the fluorescence intensity was observed (Fig. 5C) after irradiation for 7 h with a Xe lamp (500 W), which was similar to those of CQDs stored at room temperature for 3 months (Fig.5D). These results demonstrated the improved stability of CQDs. 3.2 Optimizations of reaction conditions As shown in Fig.6A, the introduction of Hg 2+ caused obviously quenching of the fluorescence of CQDs. After reaction for 10 min, the fluorescence intensity almost remained constant by further extending the reaction time, suggesting that CQDs rapidly reacted with Hg 2+ . As a result, 10 min was selected as the reaction time for the following experiments. Nevertheless, Hg 2+ had different quenching effects on the fluorescence intensity under different pH values, and the lowest fluorescence intensity achieved at pH 7.0 (Fig.6B). Furthermore, the minimum fluorescence intensity was observed as the concentration of CQDs was 5 Î¼g mL â€’1 (Fig.6C). Thus, pH 7.0 and 5 Î¼g mL â€’1 of CQDs were chosen as the optimal conditions in the following experiments. Fig.5 (A) Effects of NaCl concentration, (B) pH values, (C) irradiation time, and (D) storage time on the fluorescence intensity of CQDs Fig.6 Optimization of reaction condition: (A) reaction time, (B) pH, and (C) concentrations of CQDs XU Yue et al. / Chinese Journal of Analytical Chemistry, 2014, 42(9): 1252â€“1258 3.3 Selectivity of method As shown in Fig.7A, the selectivity of CQDs to Hg 2+ was determined by introducing interferent ions (Cd 2+ , Zn 2+ , Fe 2+ , Mn 2+ , Ba 2+ , Cu 2+ , Co 2+ , Ca 2+ , K + , Fe 3+ , Mg 2+ , Pb 2+ , Ni 2+ and Na + ) under the same conditions. Notably, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs remained almost unchanged when the concentrations of all metal ions were 50.0 Î¼M, except the presence of Fe 3+ and Cu 2+ with very slight interference (Fig.7B). The results demonstrated high selectivity of CQDs for the determination of Hg 2+ , probably owing to the stronger affinity of Hg 2+ to the carboxylic and hydroxyl on the surface of CQDs in comparison with other metal ions. Also the effects of different Hg 2+ salts (nitrate, chloride, acetate and perchlorate salt of Hg 2+ ) on the fluorescence intensity of CQDs was investigated. The results indicated that all the Hg 2+ salts display similar quenching effects. Furthermore, none of Na + salts had quenching effects on the fluorescence intensity in control experiments, demonstrating again that Hg 2+ was solely responsible for the fluorescence quenching. 3.4 Sensitivity As shown in Fig. 8A, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs gradually decreased with the Hg 2+ concentration. The fluorescence intensity displayed liner responses with the Hg 2+ concentrations, with two linear ranges of 5.0â€’100.0 nM and 1.0â€’50.0 ÂµM (Fig.8B). The linear equations are F/F 0 = 0.9827â€’0.8485C Hg2+ (R = 0.9943) and F/F 0 = 0.8565â€“0.0033 C Hg2+ (R = 0.9913), respectively. The limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be 2.3 nM (S/N = 3), indicating high sensitivity of CQDs in the present system. The comparison of CQDs with other fluorescent nanomaterial-based probes is summed in Table 1. Clearly, the developed assay had the advantages in terms of low detection limit and broad linear range. 3.5 Analysis of water samples To test practicality of the method, CQDs were extended to determine Hg 2+ in real water samples such as lake water, tap water, and underground water. All the samples were filtered through a 0.22-Âµm membrane, followed by centrifugation at 15000 rpm for 15 min to remove any suspended particles. Impressively, there was no Hg 2+ detected for these samples. The recoveries of Hg 2+ in the spiked samples were in the range of 94.6%â€’105.3%, indicating no interference of the minerals, organics, and bacteria present in the samples. In addition, the spiked samples were also detected by cold atomic absorption spectroscopy (national standards on environmental protection HJ 597-2011) for comparison. Evidently, no significant difference was found in the results obtained from the two methods (Table 2). Therefore, the developed method had great Fig.7 (A) Effects of metal ions on the fluorescence intensity of CQDs and (B) detection of Hg 2+ in the presence of excess amount of different metal ions Fig.8 Influence of Hg 2+ concentration on the fluorescence intensity of CQDs (top to bottom: 0, 0.010, 0.025, 0.050, 0.075, 0.100, 1.0, 5.0, 10.0, 25.0, and 50.0 Î¼M) XU Yue et al. / Chinese Journal of Analytical Chemistry, 2014, 42(9): 1252â€“1258 Table 1 Comparison of different fluorescent nanomaterials for determination of Hg 2+ Fluorescence methods Linear range (nM) LOD (nM) Ref. Single-labeled DNA 4â€’100 4.0 [29] BSA-Au nanoclusters 400â€’43200 80 [30] Lysozyme-Ag nanoclusters 1000â€’15000 600 [31] Glutathione-capped CdS 15â€’12500 4.5 [32] CdTe quantum dots 8â€’2000 2.7 [33] Au-cluster-CdTe nanocomposite 131â€’710 9.0 [34] CQDs 5â€’100, 1000â€’50000 2.3 This work Table 2 Analytical results of Hg 2+ in water samples potential for quantitative detection of Hg 2+ in real water samples. 4 Conclusions In summary, using apple juice as a single precursor, a one-pot hydrothermal strategy was developed for the preparation of fluorescent water soluble CQDs with good stability. A rapid detection method for Hg 2+ was developed on the basis of the quenching effect induced by Hg 2+ , and further extended to the practical assay of Hg 2+ in real water samples. The results demonstrated its practical applications in environmental monitoring and analysis. References [1] Clarkson T W, Magos L. Crit. Rev. Toxicol., 2006, 36(8): 609â€’662 [2] Li T, Dong S J, Wang E K. Anal. Chem., 2009, 81(6): 2144â€’2149 [3] Liu S J, Nie H G, Jiang J H, Shen G L, Yu R Q. Anal. ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21175118, 21275130), and the National Students' Innovation and Entrepreneurship Training Program of Zhejiang Normal University, China (No. 201310345020 for Y.X.).

head.ce:title: Green Synthesis of Fluorescent Carbon Quantum Dots for Detection of Hg2+

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Geography and Environmental Science, Zhejiang Normal University, Jinhua 321004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Geography and Environmental Science

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Life Science, Zhejiang Normal University, Jinhua 321004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Life Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescent carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were synthesized by one-step hydrothermal treatment with apple juice as raw material. The result indicated that the fluorescence could be quenched by Hg2+ with high specificity. Based on this phenomenon, a selective and sensitive sensor was constructed for the detection of Hg2+ in phosphate solutions (pH 7.0). The fluorescence intensity showed linear responses with Hg2+ concentration ranging from 5.0 to 100.0 nM and 1.0 to 50.0 μM, with the detection limit of 2.3 nM (S/N = 3). The as-fabricated sensor was further extended for the determination of Hg2+ in real water samples.

Fluorescent CQDs were synthesized with apple juice under hydrothermal conditions, which exhibited clear yellow in visible light but a bright blue fluorescence under UV light. CQDs displayed an excitation dependent emission, accompanied by the decrease of the fluorescence intensity.
